coredata.dc:title: Fast detection of tryptamine in meat products with azide-functionalized covalent organic frameworks confined in molecularly imprinted polymers
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Chenchen
He, Jianxin
Wang, Yanbo
Zhang, Dianwei
Liu, Huilin
Sun, Baoguo
coredata.dc:description: Tryptamine is a biogenic amine that affects organoleptic quality through the generation of off-odours in foods. Herein, imine-based covalent organic frameworks (COFs) were synthesized via Schiff base reactions and postmodified with click chemistry to generate azide-functionalized COFs with tunable azide units on the walls. The combination of molecular imprinting with COFs enabled the specific recognition of the targets. The resulting optosensing system (azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs) was used as a sample-to-answer analyser for detecting tryptamine (detection time within 10 min). A linear relationship was observed for the fluorescence response to tryptamine concentrations in the range of 3–120 μg L−1, with a limit of detection of 1.74 μg L−1. The recoveries for spiked samples were satisfactory, with relative standard deviations <9.90%. The optosensing system is a potential tool for the quantitative detection of tryptamine in meat products because of its lower cost, shorter processing time, and simpler processing steps compared to conventional chromatographic techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent organic frameworks
Ammonia compounds
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Azide-functionalized
Optosensing system

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TRY in real samples
Formation of an azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs optosensing system and fluorescence detection mechanism
Fluorescence sensing of TRY with azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs sensors
Analyses of TRY in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Cultivation Project of Double First-Class Disciplines of Food Science and Engineering
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272413), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFF1101101), the Cultivation Project of Double First-Class Disciplines of Food Science and Engineering, Beijing Technology & Business University (BTBU).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram showing the synthesis of azide-functionalized COFs and azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs.

Azide-functionalized COFs: (a) Scanning electron microscopy image and (b) transmission electron microscopy image. Azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs: (c) Scanning electron microscopy image and (d) transmission electron microscopy image.

(a) Fourier transform infrared spectra of TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs (black) and azide-functionalized COFs (red); (b) XPS spectra of azide-functionalized COFs. High-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy data for azide-functionalized COFs: (c) C 1 s, (d) N 1 s, and (d) O 1 s. (f) Comparative N2 (77 K) adsorption–desorption isotherms for TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs (round) and azide-functionalized COFs (square). Adsorption branches are shown in red, and desorption branches are shown in black. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence response of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs synthesized with different ratios of TRY/MAA/EGDMA to TRY (1:2:15, 1:3:15, and 1:4:15); (b) response time of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs to TRY (60 μg L−1); (c) fluorescence response curves of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs in the presence of different concentrations of TRY (3–120 μg L−1), and (d) linear plots of the fluorescence response of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs versus TRY concentration (3–120 μg L−1).

(a) Effect of TRY and its structural analogues on the fluorescence of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs; (b) quenching constants for TRY and its structural analogues on azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs and azide-functionalized COFs@NIPs; (c) fluorescence response of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs to TRY after the introduction of a TRY-azide-functionalised COF@MIP coexistence system at a high concentration of structural analogues, and (d) fluorescence response of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs to TRY after the introduction of potential interferers into the TRY-azide-functionalised COF@MIP coexistence system.
Recoveries from meat products spiked with tryptamine (TRY) using azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs.

head.ce:title: Fast detection of tryptamine in meat products with azide-functionalized covalent organic frameworks confined in molecularly imprinted polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Geriatric Nutrition and Health (Beijing Technology and Business University), Ministry of Education, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Geriatric Nutrition and Health (Beijing Technology and Business University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Geriatric Nutrition and Health (Beijing Technology and Business University), Ministry of Education, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing, 100048, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhejiang Liziyuan Food Co., LTD., China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tryptamine is a biogenic amine that affects organoleptic quality through the generation of off-odours in foods. Herein, imine-based covalent organic frameworks (COFs) were synthesized via Schiff base reactions and postmodified with click chemistry to generate azide-functionalized COFs with tunable azide units on the walls. The combination of molecular imprinting with COFs enabled the specific recognition of the targets. The resulting optosensing system (azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs) was used as a sample-to-answer analyser for detecting tryptamine (detection time within 10 min). A linear relationship was observed for the fluorescence response to tryptamine concentrations in the range of 3–120 μg L−1, with a limit of detection of 1.74 μg L−1. The recoveries for spiked samples were satisfactory, with relative standard deviations <9.90%. The optosensing system is a potential tool for the quantitative detection of tryptamine in meat products because of its lower cost, shorter processing time, and simpler processing steps compared to conventional chromatographic techniques.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tryptamine (TRY) is a biogenic amine and a low-molecular-weight nitrogen-containing organic compound produced by enzymatic carboxylations of free tryptophan and proteins (Huang et al., 2009; Zhang et al., 2019). It exists in a wide variety of foods, especially protein-rich foods such as dairy, meat, and other fermented foods (Xing, Liu, Yu, Lian, & Huang, 2012). Excessive TRY intake can cause individuals to develop low blood pressure, diarrhoea, headaches, abdominal cramps, and other related disorders. Selective monitoring of the concentrations of hazardous chemicals, such as TRY, is critical for evaluating food quality and limiting their release into the environment (Basabe-Desmonts, Reinhoudt, & Crego-Calama, 2007; Ly, Kim, & Joo, 2021). Therefore, a method with high selectivity for detecting and identifying TRY is important for agriculture and environmental security.

Mature TRY detection techniques include chromatographic methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS), capillary chromatography and ion chromatography. However, each step of the complex derivatization process can bias the results (Han, Zhu, Zhang, Liu, & Sun, 2023). Compared to chromatographic techniques, photoluminescence-based sensing systems have attracted great attention because of their significant advantages, namely, high sensitivity (Qian, Dai, Yang, & Yan, 2017; Yuan, Meng, Liu, & Sun, 2023; Zhu et al., 2018), fast response times (El-Mahdy, Lai, & Kuo, 2020), and naked-eye response (Cui et al., 2020). Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) are extended structures with predesignable lattices and periodic molecular pores (Ding & Wang, 2013). In recent years, COFs have become promising fluorescent sensing materials due to their useful structural properties and excellent fluorescence characteristics. However, the lack of specifically recognized functional groups in the pores limits their ability to recognize specific substances. The introduction of different functional units in periodic arrays via postmodification might address this challenge (Zhang, Liu, & Sun, 2023). Improving the interaction between the sensing material and the target and increasing the efficiency and specificity of the interaction between the fluorescent sensing material and the target are the main research goals.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are tailored materials that selectively attach to target analytes, similar to ‘lock and key’ methods (Villa, Sánchez, Valencia, Ahmed, & Gutiérrez, 2021). Therefore, combining fluorescent sensing materials with MIPs could improve the poor specificity (Liu et al., 2019; Xiao, Su, Teng, Hao, & Bi, 2020). Hu et al. compared the changes in fluorescence from Mg, N-CDs/Eu-MOFs and Mg, N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIPs caused by target analytes and their structural analogues under the same experimental conditions (Hu et al., 2022). Their results showed that the imprinting technique prevented interference from structural analogues during fluorescence determinations of target analytes. Unfortunately, there are still relatively few reports on combining MIPs with fluorescent COFs to improve their specificity for the detection of hazardous substances in food.

Inspired by the above ideas, azides were introduced into the precursors of imine-based COFs via click chemistry, which resulted in tunable azide units in their pore walls and improved their ability to bind to tryptamine. The azide-functionalized COFs were then conjugated to the shells of MIPs with recognition roles, and the resulting optical sensing system (azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs) was used as a “sample-to-answer” analyser for the detection of tryptamine. The resulting azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs improved the selectivity and sensitivity for TRY beyond those of nonmolecularly imprinted polymers (NIPs). In addition, the molecular recognition and signal response mechanisms of the sensing system were examined.

All reagents were of analytical grade unless otherwise stated. Among them, acetic acid, 4-azido-L-phenylalanine (33173–53-4, 99%), methacrylic acid (MAA, 79–41-4), azo diisobutyronitrile (AIBN, 78–67-1), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA, 9790–5), o-dichlorobenzene (o-DCB, 95–50-1), n-butyl alcohol (n-BuOH, 71–36-3), trichloroacetic acid (76–03-9) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 110–70-3) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. 1,3,5-tris(4-aminophenyl)benzene (TAPB, 118727–34-7, 98%), 2,5-dimethoxyterephthalaldehyde (DMTA, 7310-97-6, 97%), and 2,5-bis(proparglyoxy)terephthalaldehyde (BPTA, 1538579–23-5, 97%) were purchased from the Jilin Chinese Academy of Sciences-Yanshen Technology Co., Ltd. N,N-diisopropylethylamine, tetrahydrofuran (THF), methanol, ethanol and acetonitrile were obtained from Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd. Tryptamine (TRY, 98%), histamine (HIS, 96%), putrescine (PUT, 98%) and tyramine (TYR, 98%) were purchased from Shanghai Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water was obtained from Watsons. Jinhua ham and local sausages were purchased from Yonghui Supermarket (Beijing, China).

The morphologies of the samples were observed with a Hitachi SU 8020 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Japan) and an FEI Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a Nicolet IS10 spectrometer (USA) and were used to record the positions of the telescopic vibrational peaks of the functional groups of the synthesized materials in the spectral range of 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained by a Thermo Escalab 250 Xi (USA) spectrometer at room temperature, and the elemental compositions of the synthesized materials as well as the functional groups were analysed. Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) was conducted in the range of room temperature to 900 °C using a NETZSCH STA 449 F3 Jupiter analyser (Germany) to analyse the thermal stability of the synthesized materials. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface areas were determined at N2 (77 K) using an ASAP 2460 physical adsorber (USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded in the range of 3–30° with a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer from Germany and were used to analyse the crystalline structures of the synthesized materials. UV spectrophotometry was performed using an Agilent Cary 100 UV–-visible spectrophotometer (USA, 365 nm), and the results were used to analyse the UV absorption peaks of the synthesized materials. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a multifunctional enzyme labeler (Biotek, Winooski, SYNERGY H1, VT, USA) with an excitation wavelength of 390 nm and an emission wavelength of 420 nm.

Azide-functionalized COFs were synthesized via the method of the Xu group with slight modifications (Xu, Gao, & Jiang, 2015). DMTA (0.1 mmoL, 19.418 mg), BPTA (0.02 mmoL, 4.8446 mg), and TAPB (0.08 mmoL, 28.1 mg) were treated with o-DCB/n-BuOH (0.5/0.5 mL) in Pyrex tubes under the catalytic action of mixed acetic acid (6 M, 0.1 mL) and run through three freeze–pump–thaw cycles. The Pyrex tubes containing the mixture were sealed at 120 °C for 3 days, and the precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed six times with THF. To remove unreacted guest molecules, the collected precipitate was subjected to Soxhlet extraction with THF for 24 h. The precipitate was collected and dried overnight at 120 °C in a vacuum oven to obtain TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs.

In a 25 mL flask, 4-azido-L-phenylalanine (toluene solution, 60 μL) was added to a THF/water (2.1/0.7 mL) mixture of TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs (65 mg) in the presence of CuI (6 mg) and N,N-diisopropylethylamine (THF solution, 1 M, 108 μL). The flask was degassed with three freeze–pump–thaw cycles, and the mixture was stirred for 4 h at room temperature. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed five times with THF and acetonitrile, and dried under vacuum at room temperature to obtain azide-functionalized COFs.

The bulk polymerization methodology was based on previous work by this group. The specific steps are described below (He et al., 2023). A total of 1.5 mg of azide-functionalized COFs were placed in a 25 mL flask and distributed homogeneously in 5 mL of ethanol. The prepolymer was prepared by adding TRY (0.1 mmol, 16 mg) and MAA (0.3 mmol, 25.43 μL) and stirring for 30 min. Then, EGDMA (1.5 mmol, 282 μL) and AIBN (30 mg) were slowly added and heated on a water bath shaker at 60 °C in the dark for 24 h to obtain azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs. The template molecules (TRY) were removed by Soxhlet extraction until the UV absorption peak of TRY could no longer be detected in the eluate (methanol). The azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs with the template molecules removed were dried at 60 °C, passed through a 200-mesh sieve, and reserved. Similarly, the control azide-functionalized COFs@NIPs were prepared by using the same method, except that no TRY was added during the synthesis.

The fluorescence of the azide-functionalized COFs was assessed in terms of the fluorescence quantum yield (QY). The fluorescence QY was calculated for azide-functionalized COFs with Eq. 1, and rhodamine 6G (QY = 95%, ethanol solution) was used as a reference:
(1)
Qsam = Qref ×
A sam
A ref
×
I ref
I sam
×
n ref 2
n sam 2
where Q is the QY, A is the absorbance, I is the integrated area of the fluorescence peak, n is the refractive index, sam is the sample, and ref. is the reference substance (Grabolle et al., 2009).

To study the TRY response time of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs, the fluorescence intensity was recorded with a multifunctional enzyme standard plate to determine the response time with excitation at 390 nm and emission at 420–650 nm. An ethanol suspension of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs (1 mg/mL) was mixed with a 60 μg L−1 TRY standard solution, and the fluorescence response was detected at 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, and 20 min after mixing. In the static adsorption experiments, TRY standard solutions with different concentrations (3–120 μg L−1) were shock mixed with an ethanol suspension of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs (1 mg/mL) for at least 10 min. With excitation at 390 nm and emission at 420–650 nm, we also monitored the fluorescence of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs.

HIS, TYR, and PUT are structurally similar to TRY and were used to probe the TRY selectivity of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs. The study was performed by mixing each structural analogue (3–120 μg L−1) with the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs as well as the control group. To verify the selectivity of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs, TRY standard solutions were mixed with the structural analogues at 5× and 10× concentrations to detect fluorescence changes. The effects of several potential interferents on the selectivity of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs were also reviewed, including changes in the fluorescence intensities of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs mixed with TRY standard solution in the presence of Co2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cd+, Al3+ and amino acids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TRY in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The real samples were handled according to the national standard (GB 5009.208–2016) with minor modifications. Ten grams of a real sample was accurately weighed into a 50 mL centrifuge tube, trichloroacetic acid (50 g/L, 20 mL) and n-hexane (10 mL) were added, the mixture was mixed thoroughly, and the mixture was ultrasonicated for 30 min. After layering, the mixture was centrifuged at rest, and the supernatant was collected in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent experiments. A TRY standard solution (5, 50, or 100 μg L−1) was added to the extraction mixture, 0.5 mL of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs (ethanol solution) were mixed with 0.5 mL of the extraction mixture, and the fluorescence intensity was measured.

All the data are presented as the means of at least three replicates unless otherwise stated. The variability of the data was assessed via analyses performed with SPSS Statistics 17, with p < 0.05 indicating a statistically significant difference. Figures were plotted with Origin 2018.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of an azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs optosensing system and fluorescence detection mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of azide-functionalized COFs and the imprinting process and recognition principle of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs are shown in Fig. 1 . TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs were prepared via the condensation of three monomers, DMTA, TAPB, and BPTA, with a reversible Schiff base reaction and heating. DMTA is rich in methoxy groups, which strengthen the interlayer interactions and result in excellent stability and high crystallinity. Moreover, since DMTA contains hydrogen bonds that make the structure rigid, it is an efficient fluorophore either in the solid-state or when dispersed in solution (Hayashi, Koizumi, & Kamiya, 2019). Therefore, the TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs synthesized from monomeric DMTA exhibited strong fluorescence and stability. The TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs were postmodified with 4-azido-L-phenylalanine, which contains an azide group, via the Huisgen cycloaddition reaction, and the excitation and emission wavelengths of the modified azide-functionalized COFs were 400 nm and 490 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). The QY of the azide-functionalized COFs was 9.87%. As shown in Fig. S2, the UV absorption wavelength of the prepared azide-functionalized COFs was 280 nm, and the UV absorption spectrum of the azide-functionalized COFs did not change after the introduction of TRY. By examining the distance between the fluorescence emission peaks for the azide-functionalized COFs (490 nm) and the UV absorption spectrum of TRY (220 nm), common fluorescence quenching mechanisms, such as Förster energy resonance transfer (FRET), static quenching (SQE), and the internal filter effect (IFE), were excluded. Therefore, we hypothesized that the mechanism leading to changes in the fluorescence of the azide-functionalized COFs was photoinduced electron transfer (PET). In other words, when azide-functionalized COFs act as electron donors to TRY, the excited electrons cannot return to their original orbitals and are placed in the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of TRY, resulting in fluorescence quenching (Yuan et al., 2023; X. C. Zhu, Yuan, Han, Liu, & Sun, 2021).

Azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs were prepared with azide-functionalized COFs as fluorescence reporter elements; TRY, MAA, and EGDMA as template molecules; functional monomers; and cross-linkers. In this process, TRY and MAA were prepolymerized in ethanol by van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding, and EGDMA initiated double-bond polymerization to form a polymer with the luminescent material embedded in it. The TRY molecules were removed by breaking the hydrogen bonds on the polymer surface with a strongly polar solvent (methanol) to obtain azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs with three-dimensional cavities on the surfaces that specifically bound the TRY molecules, which again captured and recognized the TRY molecules. The excitation and emission wavelengths of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs were 390 nm and 450 nm, respectively (Fig. S3). In the fluorescence determinations, the original optimal emission wavelength for the azide-functionalized COFs was 490 nm. Due to bonding between some reagents used in the molecularly imprinted synthesis and the functional groups on the surfaces of the azide-functionalized COFs, as well as physical embedding of the molecularly imprinted layer on the luminescent centres, the emission wavelength of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs was blueshifted from 490 nm to 450 nm.

The morphology of the azide-functionalized COFs was observed by SEM and TEM. The azide-functionalized COFs had spherical structures, and NanoDrop 1.2 was used to analyse the particle sizes; the average size was 72.5 nm (Fig. 2a). A distinct lattice structure with a spacing of 0.88 nm was observed in the TEM images (Fig. 2b). The average particle size of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs was determined to be 420 nm (Fig. 2c, inset). Due to the cross-linking agent, these spheres were interlaced and overlapped (Fig. 2d). SEM images showed that the polymer surface had a rough structure due to the presence of imprinted pores remaining after the removal of TRY.

The syntheses of these materials were demonstrated by FT-IR spectroscopy, which was used to determine the functional groups and chemical bonds on the surfaces of TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs and the azide-functionalized COFs (Fig. 3a). Four characteristic peaks were observed in the FT-IR spectrum of the TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs, which was in general agreement with the literature (Xu et al., 2015). The stretching vibrational peaks at 1694 cm−1 and 1643 cm−1 were attributed to CC and CN, the peak at 2130 cm−1 was assigned to CC, and the peak at 3300 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C ≡ C-H, which indicated that the TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs were prepared. The peaks exhibited after modification of the TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs with the introduction of azides were approximately the same as those of the TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs, indicating that the azides were grafted onto the TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs without destroying the original structure of the TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs. XPS was used to confirm the modification of the azide substance (Fig. 3b-e). XPS revealed the elemental composition of the azide-functionalized COFs, and the presence of CN was identified in the deconvolved C 1 s and N 1 s spectra, which coincided with the FT-IR spectra, proving that the introduction of the azide substance did not change the original structure of the TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs. As shown in Table S1, the N content in the modified COFs increased from 6.93% to 12.18%, which was due to the formation of triazole units consisting of four N atoms in the click chemistry reaction; thus, the elemental content of N in the azide-functionalized COFs increased dramatically (Martinez-Fernandez et al., 2022).

The FT-IR absorption peaks for the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs and azide-functionalized COFs@NIPs were essentially the same (Fig. S4a). The characteristic peak for the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs was observed in four absorption peaks associated with bulk polymerization. These peaks included the stretching vibrational peaks for the -CH2 (2985 cm−1) and -CH3 (2958 cm−1) groups in EGDMA; the absorption peak at 1706 cm−1, which was attributed to a CO stretch; and the strong absorption at 1235 cm−1, which was attributed to the CO stretching vibration. These peaks demonstrated that the polymer was synthesized and that the ester group of MAA was successfully grafted onto the polymer surface.

The crystallinities of the two synthesized COFs were examined by XRD (Fig. S4b). The TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs exhibited diffraction peak positions similar to those in the literature (Xu et al., 2015), and the diffraction peaks for the azide-functionalized COFs after postmodification were similar to those of the precursor COFs, indicating that introduction of the azide substituents did not significantly change the crystallinity of the COFs. The lattice structure of the azide-functionalized COFs confirmed this conclusion. No diffraction peak for azide-functionalized COFs was found in the XRD pattern for the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs (Fig. S4c), which indicated that the azide-functionalized COFs were encapsulated by the polymer. The new broad diffraction peak at 10–15° indicated an amorphous structure, suggesting successful synthesis of the polymer.

The thermal stabilities of the azide-functionalized COFs and azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs were evaluated via thermogravimetric (TG) analyses (Fig. S4d). The azide-functionalized COFs lost only 11.49% of their mass in the temperature range from room temperature to 400 °C, which might have been caused by evaporation of the residual solvent molecules (Ni et al., 2018). When the temperature was increased further to 900 °C, the total weight loss reached 36.75%, which was due to disintegration and cleavage of the macromolecular skeleton in the azide-functionalized COFs. The azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs showed good thermal stability below 300 °C, and the mass loss was only 5.94%. As the temperature increased further, the thermal decomposition rate accelerated sharply, and the structure of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs was destroyed. Both of these materials exhibited excellent stability below 300 °C, meeting the requirements of practical applications.

The effect of azide on the porosity of the TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs was evaluated with nitrogen sorption isotherms (Fig. 3f). Both the TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs and azide-functionalized COFs exhibited the obvious type IV isotherms of mesoporous materials. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area and pore volume of the TAPB-BPTA-DMTA-COFs changed due to the introduction of azide. The BET surface area decreased from 2028.23 m2 g−1 to 827.55 m2 g−1, and the pore volume decreased from 0.866 cm3 g−1 to 0.312 cm3 g−1. These data were consistent with those reported by Xu et al. (Xu et al., 2015).

As the fluorescence reporter element in the optosensing system, the added azide-functionalized COFs were a key factor. Fig. S5 shows the effects of azide-functionalized COFs with different amounts of added COFs (between 0.5 and 2.5 mg) on the fluorescence intensity of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs. The fluorescence intensity increased with increasing amounts of azide-functionalized COFs from 0.5 mg to 1.5 mg; when the amount of azide-functionalized COFs added exceeded 1.5 mg, the fluorescence intensity of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs decreased, which was attributed to the fact that imine-based COFs usually feature strong interlayer π-π stacking, which leads to aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the addition of 1.5 mg was a better choice for the construction of the optosensing system.

The ratios of the template molecules, functional monomers, and crosslinkers affected the binding sites for TRY on the surface of the MIPs and thus affected the ability of the polymer to detect and trap TRY. Therefore, TRY/MAA/EGDMA ratios of 1:2:15, 1:3:15, and 1:4:15 were used to study the effects of different TRY concentrations on the fluorescence quenching of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs (Fig. 4a). It was evident that the synthesized polymer had a significant response to TRY at a TRY:MAA:EGDMA ratio of 1:3:15. Therefore, this ratio was used in subsequent experiments.

The stability of the fluorescence from the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs in solution affected their response to TRY. The azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs were dispersed in water, methanol, ethanol, and acetonitrile for 100 min to monitor their fluorescence intensities (Fig. S6). The fluorescence intensity of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs was the highest in acetonitrile, but the fluorescence intensity changed more significantly with time, while the fluorescence intensity was most stable in ethanol. Therefore, ethanol was chosen as the incubation solvent for the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of TRY with azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The dynamic adsorption equilibrium time visually reflected the binding rate of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs sensor with TRY. The equilibrium time directly affected the mixed oscillation time of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs and TRY in subsequent experiments. As shown in Fig. 4b, quenching of the fluorescence from the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs gradually increased over the first 10 min, while adsorption equilibrium was reached after 10 min. Therefore, 10 min was determined to be the minimum time required to complete binding of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs sensor with TRY, and the next experiments were performed with 10 min as the optimal adsorption time.

To confirm the binding of the constructed azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs sensor with TRY, the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs sensor was exposed to TRY standard solutions with different concentrations. Fig. 4c shows that quenching (F 0 /F) of the fluorescence from the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs was more effective when the TRY concentration was higher. The degree of quenching (F 0 /F) and TRY concentration were analysed with the Stern–Volmer equation, Eq. 2:
(2)
F 0
/ F = K sv C + 1
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs before and after binding to TRY, respectively; Ksv is the Stern–Volmer constant; and C is the concentration of the TRY standard solution. In this study, the concentration of the TRY standard solution showed a clear linear relationship with quenching of the fluorescence of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs at 3–120 μg L−1 (Fig. 4d). The corresponding standard equation was F 0 /F = 0.00221C + 1.17, R2 = 0.987, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.74 μg L−1 (3S/N) (Yuan, Jiang, Wang, Liu, & Sun, 2020). The quenching of the fluorescence from the azide-functionalized COFs@NIPs was not significantly affected by the standard equation F 0 /F = 0.000601C + 1.03, R2 = 0.989 (Fig. S7). The imprinting factor (IF=Ksvazide-functionalized COFs@MIPs: Ksvazide-functionalized COFs@NIPs) was an important indicator of the ability of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs to bind TRY. Under the optimal conditions, the IF was 3.68, showing that the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs captured TRY efficiently, which may have been due to the presence of imprinted cavities in the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs that bound TRY, which was not found with the azide-functionalized COFs@NIPs.

The selectivity of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs was evaluated with histamine (HIS), tyramine (TYR), and putrescine (PUT), which contain similar functional groups or structures, along with their effects on the fluorescence of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs. The structural formulas of TRY and its analogues are shown in Fig. S8. At the same concentrations, quenching of TRY fluorescence by the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs was significantly more efficient than quenching of the fluorescence of the three analogues HIS, TYR, and PUT (Fig. 5a). The Ksv values for TRY and its analogues also verified the high TRY selectivity of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs sensor (Fig. 5b). This occurred because the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs had imprinted pores with the same sizes and structures as TRY, which were formed during synthesis; these pores selectively and efficiently trapped and adsorbed TRY.

Furthermore, the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs retained their original response to TRY fluorescence when TRY was combined with high concentrations of the analogues (5× and 10×) (Fig. 5c), suggesting that the analogues were not bound to the imprinted pore cavities that specifically recognized TRY and thus determined the nanomaterial reactions. Since some interfering substances may have still been present even after sample treatment and affected the accuracy of the sensor, potential interferents, e.g., ions and amino acids, were added to the system in the presence of 120 μg L−1 TRY to probe interference with TRY detection. The fluorescence response of the system with added substances was similar to that of the blank group without interfering substances (Fig. 5d), demonstrating the high selectivity and specificity of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs sensor.

The azide-functionalized COFs were immersed in different solvents (water, DMF, methanol, and THF) for one week to determine their chemical stabilities. The FT-IR spectra revealed that the azide-functionalized COFs retained their original chemical structures (Fig. S9), and their chemical stability was satisfactory.

The fluorescence stability of the sensor was judged by changes in the fluorescence intensity caused by preservation time and exposure time. The results showed that the fluorescence intensities of the azide-functionalized COFs and azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs did not change significantly during continuous exposure to UV lamps (365 nm), and both showed excellent resistance to photobleaching (Fig. S10a and b). During storage for up to one month, the fluorescence intensities of the azide-functionalized COFs and azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs were similar to those of the initial COFs, indicating that the nanomaterials had satisfactory fluorescence stability (Fig. S10c and d).

Reproducibility is a prominent advantage of MIP nanomaterials, and readsorption can be achieved by removing the template molecules (TRY) on the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs with polar solvents. After four adsorption-elution cycles with the nanomaterials, the fluorescence response of the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs to TRY was slightly reduced by multiple binding and separation cycles, which was likely due to the difficulty in separating TRY from the interior. Interestingly, the azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs retained 93.91% of the initial fluorescence response to TRY after four cycles, demonstrating the excellent reusability of the constructed azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs sensor (Fig. S11).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses of TRY in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Accurate identification and capture of hazardous substances in food is challenging due to the complexity of the food matrix. However, MIPs capable of targeted recognition combined with fluorescent elements that transform the contents of hazardous substances in food into optical signals can provide quantitative detection. To verify the applicability of the constructed optical nanomaterial sensor in complex food matrices, Jinhua ham and local sausages were selected for spiked recovery analyses. The recovery rates ranged from 86.63 to 107.96%, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) of <9.90% (Table 1 ). The practicality of the optical nanomaterial sensor was verified by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The fitting curves for the TRY standard solution, chromatograms of the TRY standard solution, and chromatograms of the local sausages are shown in Fig. S12a-c. Based on the fitting curves and chromatograms, the spiked recovery rates of the samples were 84.97–92.78%, with an RSD of <2.3% (Table S2). These two results were consistent, and the feasibility of the constructed optosensing system was verified.

The linear ranges, limits of detection, recovery rates, and sample matrices reported in recent articles on the detection of TRY are listed in Table S3 to assess the advantages of the constructed optosensing system. This system had a lower LOD of 1.74 μg L−1 than another optosensing system of the same type, and this system was more practical because it required simpler pretreatment of the sample than HPLC, did not require TRY derivatization, and did not require great skill from the operator.

We constructed light-emitting azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs via one-pot bulk polymerization for the detection of trace TRY in meat products. The prepared azide-functionalized COFs showed a high BET surface area, high stability from room temperature to 300 °C, and high fluorescence quantum yields. Azide-functionalized COFs@MIPs can be used as integrated recognition-response materials with good linearity in the range of 3–120 μg L−1 and high sensitivity and specificity for tryptamine, with an LOD of 1.74 μg L−1. The recovery rates for spiked meat products ranged from 86.63 to 107.96%, with an RSD of <9.9%. In addition, the fabricated sensing system showed excellent stability, reliability, reusability, and practicability, indicating its potential for monitoring trace amine compounds in food matrices. However, its portability requires further improvement before it can be used for the rapid testing of samples in the field.

Chenchen Xie: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Jianxin He: Methodology. Yanbo Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources. Dianwei Zhang: Supervision, Conceptualization. Huilin Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Baoguo Sun: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272413), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFF1101101), the Cultivation Project of Double First-Class Disciplines of Food Science and Engineering, Beijing Technology & Business University (BTBU).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.139527.
